summary: Continuous Delivery is the practice of building software that can be released to production at any time through extensive automation and collaboration. It requires maintaining deployable software throughout its lifecycle, using deployment pipelines, and fostering DevOps culture. The post distinguishes Continuous Delivery from Continuous Deployment—the former means you can deploy frequently, while the latter means every change automatically goes to production. Key benefits include reduced deployment risk through smaller changes, more believable progress metrics, and faster user feedback loops.
key_points: Software must be deployable throughout its lifecycle, with teams prioritizing deployability over new features
Continuous Delivery requires a deployment pipeline with automated testing and increasingly production-like environments
Continuous Deployment is a subset of Continuous Delivery—all Continuous Deployment requires Continuous Delivery, but not vice versa
DevOps culture must extend beyond developers and operations to include testers, database teams, and everyone involved in delivery
User feedback is the greatest risk mitigator, as it reveals whether you're building something actually useful
money_quotes: The key test is that a business sponsor could request that the current development version of the software can be deployed into production at a moment's notice - and nobody would bat an eyelid, let alone panic.
Your team prioritizes keeping the software deployable over working on new features
If 'done' means 'developers declare it to be done' that's much less believable than if it's deployed into a production (or production-like) environment.
The biggest risk to any software effort is that you end up building something that isn't useful.
Since you are deploying smaller changes, there's less to go wrong and it's easier to fix should a problem appear.
key_insight: True readiness for production deployment—not just developer sign-off—is the only believable measure of software progress and the key to reducing both technical and business risk.
